facilities.title: Pusat Jagaan Al Fikrah Malaysia
facilities.editorial:
Pusat Jagaan Al-Fikrah Malaysia operates from Lot 683, Jalan Sekolah, Kampung Sungai Sekamat — a kampung enclave off Jalan Cheras in Kajang, Selangor. The home was established in August 2013 as a non-governmental welfare organisation run under a committee chaired by Haji Mohd Azmi Bin Mohd Zain. It carries Islamic values as a core institutional principle and serves a mix of populations: elderly residents without family support, chronically ill individuals, converts to Islam, people with disabilities, and individuals discharged from hospital with no one to receive them at home.

Al-Fikrah Malaysia is a welfare home rather than a fee-based nursing facility. Its funding model relies on public donations and contributions from volunteers rather than monthly resident fees, which means its intake process and eligibility criteria are managed very differently from private nursing homes. Families with a relative who has no existing family support structure and who meets the welfare criteria may be eligible; families seeking a private paid placement should be aware that Al-Fikrah operates outside the commercial nursing home market. The home holds a 4.5-star Google rating from 95 reviews — a meaningful base in the context of a small Kajang-area welfare home — and the volume of reviewers suggests that the home is well known and appreciated in the surrounding community.

For families or social workers exploring options for a relative with no family support, the correct first step is to contact the home directly to understand current capacity and the intake process. Questions worth raising on that call: what are the eligibility criteria for admission, whether there is a waiting list, and whether the home holds a current JKM Akta Pusat Jagaan licence (welfare homes operate on different regulatory tracks from licensed nursing homes, and the distinction matters for care oversight). Al-Fikrah is not the right referral for families seeking a private, fee-paying placement, but for the population it serves — isolated elderly individuals without family, newly converted Muslims, or those discharged from hospital into homelessness — it represents an important and under-resourced community asset.

facilities.title: Mega Senior Care Centre
facilities.editorial:
Mega Senior Care Centre is located at 29 Jalan Pantai 9/7, Seksyen 9, Petaling Jaya — a shophouse unit on a quiet residential street in one of PJ's older and most established neighbourhoods. Despite the Google Maps area tag reading "Sek 9", the address places it firmly in Petaling Jaya rather than Shah Alam, making it accessible for families in the Pantai-PJ corridor as well as residents of the adjacent Bangsar South and Kerinchi areas. Specific details — founding year, operator name and JKM licence number — are not stated in the home's public listings.

The centre holds a 5.0-star Google rating from 35 reviews. While the review base is moderate in size, a perfect score without a single low-rating outlier indicates a consistent pattern of satisfied visitors and family members. Pricing is not published and is quoted on direct enquiry. Services are not detailed in public listings, so the scope of clinical care, medical staffing, and the types of care conditions accepted are all points to clarify when you call. The shophouse format is worth noting: single-storey converted shophouses work well for ambulatory residents but can be limiting for those who are bedbound or require ceiling hoists, so the physical layout is worth asking about if your relative has significant mobility needs.

When you contact Mega Senior Care Centre, ask for the JKM Akta Pusat Jagaan licence number and status, the nurse-to-resident ratio for day and night shifts, and whether the home accepts residents with dementia or who require tube feeding. Confirm the pricing model — whether it is monthly all-inclusive or itemises extras such as medication, physiotherapy and incontinence supplies separately. Ask how many residents are currently accommodated. If the shophouse has multiple floors, confirm whether lift access is available. A site visit before committing to placement is strongly recommended.

facilities.title: KV Guardian Senior Care Centre (Nursing Home)
facilities.editorial:
KV Guardian Senior Care Centre occupies a residential property at No. 6 Jalan Persiaran Titiwangsa 3, in the Taman Tasik Titiwangsa pocket — a low-density residential area adjacent to the Titiwangsa lake park in central Kuala Lumpur. The operator describes itself as a family-owned home, and markets the Titiwangsa site as both a senior care centre and a nursing home. A company website is published, but founding year, ownership details and JKM licence number are not stated there publicly.

The publicly described service scope spans assisted living, rehabilitation, medical nursing care, and retirement-care stays — a range typical of private KL nursing homes in this tier, covering everything from low-dependency assisted living through to post-hospital medical nursing. The extent to which specialist clinical services are actually staffed and available on-site, as opposed to being listed in general terms, is worth verifying directly. The 4.3-star Google rating from 34 reviews is a solid mid-range result and does not suggest any particular pattern of concern. Pricing is quoted on enquiry rather than published. The lake park setting and the residential character of the Titiwangsa neighbourhood provide a calmer environment than many nursing homes in busier commercial corridors.

The Titiwangsa location has practical advantages for families based in or near central KL: Hospital Kuala Lumpur is approximately three kilometres to the north, and the Titiwangsa LRT, MRT and monorail interchange makes the home accessible without a car. When you contact KV Guardian, ask for the JKM Akta Pusat Jagaan licence number and expiry date, the nurse-to-resident ratio for overnight shifts, and which clinical conditions — dementia, PEG tube feeding, palliative care — are accepted. Also confirm whether the specific unit you visit is the one you are being offered, as family-owned operators sometimes run multiple properties under a shared brand name.

facilities.title: Pusat Jagaan dan Rawatan Orang Tua Al-Ikhlas
facilities.editorial:
Pusat Jagaan dan Rawatan Orang Tua Al-Ikhlas is located inside a converted endowed mosque — a masjid lama diwakafkan — at Lot 3530, Jalan Batu 18, Kampung Pulau Meranti, Puchong. This kampung enclave sits between Puchong's commercial sprawl and the Putrajaya wetlands, providing a quieter, village-like setting than most Puchong nursing homes that occupy shophouse or terrace-row premises. The home was founded in 1999 by Muji binti Hj Sulaiman, a former Chief Nurse at Hospital Serdang, who began by bringing home elderly patients discharged from the hospital with no family to receive them.

The founder's clinical background shapes the home's character in a meaningful way. Muji binti Hj Sulaiman's decades of hospital nursing experience gives Al-Ikhlas an operational ethos grounded in medical understanding rather than simply domestic supervision — a distinction that matters when evaluating the credibility of care claims. The home is Islamic in character; residents are housed in a converted mosque building, which brings a unique physical environment and a spiritual dimension that many Muslim families find appropriate. The 4.4-star Google rating from 34 reviews is solid for an establishment of this scale in the Puchong area. Pricing is not published and is quoted directly on contact.

For families considering Al-Ikhlas, the founding story matters: this home began as an informal welfare response to discharged hospital patients and grew from that foundation. That origin suggests a care commitment grounded in genuine need rather than purely commercial motivation. On your initial call, ask about the JKM Akta Pusat Jagaan licence status, current resident capacity and availability, nursing qualifications and staffing ratios, and which care conditions — bedbound residents, dementia, palliative — are accepted. The Puchong location is well positioned for families across the Puchong-Putrajaya-Cyberjaya belt, with the IOI City corridor approximately fifteen minutes away.

facilities.title: Pusat Jagaan Chik Sin Thong Klang Dan Pantai Selangor
facilities.editorial:
Pusat Jagaan Chik Sin Thong is a Chinese-community charity home in Klang, run by The Association of Klang and Coast Chik Sin Thong Old Folks Home Selangor. The main site sits on Persiaran Tengku Ampuan Rahimah at Batu 1¼, on the edge of Taman Sri Andalas — one of Klang's established residential neighbourhoods, well connected for family visits. Chik Sin Thong is a long-standing Chinese welfare association; this home is one of its key charitable services, operating as a non-profit that relies on public donations rather than resident fee income.

The home issues regular public appeals for adult diapers, toiletries, fresh produce and everyday consumables — a pattern common to community charity homes and a transparent signal of how the budget is structured. Admission follows a welfare rather than commercial model: families or individuals seeking a private fee-paying placement should be aware that intake may depend on the committee's assessment of need and current occupancy rather than a straightforward application-and-pay process. The 4.1-star Google rating from 32 reviews is modest but consistent with how charity welfare homes are typically reviewed; community donor visits and volunteering visits make up much of the review pool rather than fee-paying consumer comparisons.

For families with a relative who may qualify under the home's welfare criteria — typically elderly individuals with Chinese community ties, limited family support, or financial hardship — the direct approach is to contact the association committee and explain the situation. Relevant questions include: what the current occupancy is, what care conditions and levels of physical dependency are accommodated, whether nursing staff are resident on-site or visiting, and what the JKM Akta Pusat Jagaan licensing status is. Families expecting a private-pay arrangement with itemised monthly pricing should look at licensed commercial nursing homes in Klang instead.

facilities.title: Serene Skies Care Centre Nursing Home
facilities.editorial:
Serene Skies Care Centre occupies a property in Taman Seri Subang — a residential neighbourhood directly adjacent to the Bandar Sunway township — and markets itself to families across the Sunway-Subang Jaya-Puchong-Kelana Jaya catchment area. The Sunway location is convenient: Sunway Medical Centre is minutes away, Subang Jaya Medical Centre is reachable within fifteen minutes, and the area is well connected via the Kelana Jaya LRT extension. The home describes itself as a nursing home and offers services spanning assisted living through to nursing-level care, though the specific clinical capabilities are best confirmed directly.

The centre holds a 5.0-star Google rating from 30 reviews — a clean score without a low-rating outlier, indicating a consistent pattern of satisfied visitors and family members. Pricing is not published and is quoted on enquiry. The operating entity, staff qualifications and founding year are not detailed in public listings, so those details need to be gathered during a direct conversation with management. The Taman Seri Subang address places the home in Selangor, which matters for families comparing options across the PJ-Sunway border with KL-registered facilities — the regulatory and referral networks differ slightly by state.

When you contact Serene Skies, ask for the JKM Akta Pusat Jagaan licence number and its validity date, the current resident count and bed capacity, and the nurse-to-resident staffing ratios for day and night shifts. Confirm which care conditions are accepted — dementia, palliative, PEG tube, post-stroke rehabilitation — as the website language is general. Ask whether the monthly fee is all-inclusive or whether medication, physiotherapy and incontinence supplies are itemised separately. A site visit to view the rooms, common areas and outdoor space before placing a family member is always worth arranging.

facilities.title: Tai Kuk Wah Si Buddhist Society
facilities.editorial:
Tai Kuk Wah Si Buddhist Society is primarily a Buddhist religious organisation based in Taman Pertama, Cheras — a neighbourhood along the Selangor-KL boundary corridor on the Cheras-Balakong axis. The society's elder care home is attached to its temple complex and operates as a welfare extension of the religious mission rather than as a commercial nursing facility. With 20 listed beds, the home is modest in scale; the public profile is minimal, with a phone number as the only readily available contact and no independent website or service brochure.

The welfare character of this home is the key framing for families to understand. As a Buddhist society welfare arm, the home most likely applies a charitable admission model — prioritising elderly individuals who are Buddhist, connected to the association's community, or without family support — rather than accepting all-comers on a fee-paying basis. Funding comes from the religious society and community donations rather than resident fees. The 4.6-star Google rating from 29 reviews is consistent with a well-regarded community institution; this kind of score from a small, community-embedded welfare home typically reflects genuine appreciation from volunteers, donors and community members rather than a consumer comparison audience. No pricing is published, which is consistent with a welfare model.

For families who have a connection to this Buddhist community — particularly elderly relatives who are practising Buddhists or who have ties to the Cheras Chinese community — a direct phone call to the society is the right first step. Ask whether the home currently has capacity, what the admission criteria are, and what JKM licensing status applies. Families who do not have a community connection and who are looking for a private fee-paying placement in the Cheras area will find a better fit in the licensed commercial nursing homes operating in the corridor.

facilities.title: Persatuan Kebajikan Dan Social Kim Loo Ting
facilities.editorial:
Persatuan Kebajikan Dan Social Kim Loo Ting — 甘露亭 — is a Buddhist welfare association operating a residential home for elderly individuals in Taman Ayer Panas, Setapak, on the Selangor-KL fringe. The home follows a specific charitable model: it primarily admits elderly residents who have been discharged from government hospitals with no family support and no fixed address, providing shelter and ongoing care for people who have genuinely nowhere else to go. Capacity is listed at 25 beds, placing it among the smaller welfare homes in the greater KL area.

Kim Loo Ting's admission model is narrow by design. Because the home targets a specific welfare population — elderly homeless individuals or those abandoned at hospital discharge — families looking for a nursing home placement for a parent or relative who has family support are unlikely to be eligible under this home's criteria. The 4.7-star Google rating from 28 reviews reflects a small but appreciative community: likely volunteers, donors and community members familiar with the society's welfare work rather than families making a consumer comparison. No pricing is published, consistent with the home's non-commercial welfare mandate.

For social workers, hospital discharge planners or community volunteers managing cases of elderly individuals without any family support in the KL-Setapak-Selangor border area, Kim Loo Ting is a meaningful referral point. Direct contact is essential; ask about current capacity and the intake process. For families with a relative who has family support and is actively looking for a care placement, this home is not the appropriate referral. Families in Setapak or Ayer Panas seeking a private licensed nursing home should instead look at commercial facilities in the area that operate under the Akta Pusat Jagaan framework.

facilities.title: PJ Mentalink Care (PJ Old Folks)
facilities.editorial:
PJ Mentalink Care — also operating under the names Pusat Jagaan Mentalink and Roy Comfort Care Centre — is a Petaling Jaya-based operator with an unusual history: the group was founded in 1998 originally as a private psychiatric residential care service, and subsequently expanded to include an elderly care arm. The flagship elderly home operates from 109 Jalan Templer, Section 8 PJ — a 20-bed house located a short distance from University Malaya Medical Centre (PPUM). The group reports running eight homes in total across PJ, covering both the psychiatric and elderly care segments.

The psychiatric origins of the group signal a clinical background in residential care management that goes beyond typical aged care operators. Families considering the elderly home specifically should verify that the staffing and care protocols at the elderly arm are appropriate for their relative's needs, since the primary clinical expertise of the group may be weighted toward psychiatric rather than geriatric care — and those two populations have different medical, nutritional and mobility requirements. The 4.0-star Google rating from 27 reviews is moderate, suggesting a mixed pool of experiences. Pricing is not published. The proximity to PPUM is a practical advantage for residents with complex medical needs who may require hospital transfers.

The Section 8 PJ location suits families based across the University-PJ-Bangsar corridor. When you contact PJ Mentalink, ask specifically about the JKM Akta Pusat Jagaan licence status for the elderly home, the nursing qualifications and overnight coverage for the elderly care unit, and which care conditions — dementia, post-stroke, palliative — are accepted. Because the operator runs multiple properties under related names, confirm that the home you visit is the specific site your relative would be placed in, and ask about how care is coordinated if residents across different homes need different levels of support.

facilities.title: Meridian Care Living Centre
facilities.editorial:
Meridian Care Living Centre operates from a residential property at 31 Jalan Abdullah in Bangsar — a central KL address in one of the city's most accessible and well-serviced neighbourhoods. The home markets itself as an enhanced assisted-living and rehabilitation centre rather than a conventional nursing home, and serves both longer-term elder care residents and shorter-term post-hospital or post-surgical recovery placements. A daycare programme is also offered, making the centre an option for families who are not yet at the point of full-time residential placement but who need structured daytime supervision and care.

The care team is described as a mix of Malaysian and foreign nurses qualified to BSN (Bachelor of Science in Nursing) level and registered with the appropriate professional body — a staffing claim worth confirming on your visit by asking to see relevant registration certificates. The 4.6-star Google rating from 26 reviews is solid for a Bangsar-area facility of this scale. Pricing is not published and is quoted on enquiry, which is standard for Bangsar-market residential care facilities where packages tend to be more customised than in volume-based nursing homes. The address puts the centre within close reach of Pantai Medical Centre, Hospital Universiti Malaya (PPUM) and Damansara Specialist Hospital — a practical advantage for residents requiring specialist follow-up or emergency transfer.

When you contact Meridian Care, ask for the JKM Akta Pusat Jagaan licence number and expiry date, the current number of residents and room configuration, and the nursing qualifications of the care team on overnight duty. Confirm which conditions are accepted beyond assisted living — dementia, palliative, PEG tube, post-stroke rehabilitation — and how medication management is handled. For families in or near Bangsar who want a smaller, higher staff-to-resident ratio environment close to specialist hospitals, Meridian Care is positioned in the right segment of the market.
